title: AutoCAD Complete Shortcut Commands Reference
description: A comprehensive reference of all important AutoCAD keyboard shortcuts, function keys, and command aliases. Includes what each shortcut does, how to use it, and practical tips for beginners.

categories.description: Function keys are single-key toggles that turn important drafting tools on and off instantly.

shortcuts.name: Help
shortcuts.description: Opens the AutoCAD Help documentation.
shortcuts.howToUse: Press F1 at any time to open the Help window. Search for any command or topic you do not understand.
shortcuts.tip: Very useful for beginners. If you are unsure what a command does, press F1 and search for it.

shortcuts.name: Text Window / Command History
shortcuts.description: Opens the full command history window showing all previously typed commands.
shortcuts.howToUse: Press F2 to open the expanded text window. Scroll up to see every command you have used in the current session.
shortcuts.tip: Useful for reviewing what went wrong if your drawing behaves unexpectedly.

shortcuts.name: Object Snap (OSNAP) Toggle
shortcuts.description: Turns Object Snap on or off. Object Snap snaps the cursor to exact points like endpoints, midpoints, and centers.
shortcuts.howToUse: Press F3 to toggle OSNAP on or off while drawing. Right-click the OSNAP button in the status bar to open settings and choose which snap points to enable.
shortcuts.tip: Keep OSNAP ON at all times during drawing. Walls, lines, and objects only connect correctly when OSNAP is active.

shortcuts.name: 3D Object Snap Toggle
shortcuts.description: Turns 3D Object Snap on or off. Only relevant when working in 3D.
shortcuts.howToUse: Press F4 to toggle. Not commonly needed for standard 2D municipal drawings.
shortcuts.tip: For 2D municipal drawings, you can leave this off.

shortcuts.name: Isoplane Toggle
shortcuts.description: Switches between isometric drawing planes — Top, Left, and Right.
shortcuts.howToUse: Press F5 to cycle through isometric planes when drawing isometric views.
shortcuts.tip: Only needed when preparing isometric plumbing or piping diagrams. Not used in standard floor plans.

shortcuts.name: Dynamic UCS Toggle
shortcuts.description: Turns Dynamic User Coordinate System on or off. Useful in 3D work.
shortcuts.howToUse: Press F6 to toggle. Mostly used in 3D modeling.
shortcuts.tip: Not needed for standard 2D municipal drawings. Leave it off.

shortcuts.name: Grid Display Toggle
shortcuts.description: Turns the visual grid dots or lines on or off in the drawing area.
shortcuts.howToUse: Press F7 to show or hide the grid. The grid does not print — it is a visual reference only.
shortcuts.tip: Some draftsmen turn the grid off for a cleaner drawing environment. It is a personal preference.

shortcuts.name: Ortho Mode Toggle
shortcuts.description: Forces the cursor to move only horizontally or vertically. Prevents diagonal lines when drawing straight walls.
shortcuts.howToUse: Press F8 to turn Ortho on or off. When ON, you can only draw perfectly straight horizontal and vertical lines.
shortcuts.tip: Keep Ortho ON when drawing walls, columns, and grids. Turn it OFF only when you need to draw a diagonal element like a ramp or angled wall.

shortcuts.name: Snap Mode Toggle
shortcuts.description: Turns cursor Snap to grid on or off. Forces the cursor to jump to fixed grid intervals.
shortcuts.howToUse: Press F9 to toggle. Right-click the Snap button in the status bar to set the snap interval distance.
shortcuts.tip: Object Snap (F3) is more useful than Grid Snap (F9) for most municipal drawing work. Most professionals leave F9 off.

shortcuts.name: Polar Tracking Toggle
shortcuts.description: Guides the cursor along specific angles — typically 45° and 90° increments — with a visual tracking line.
shortcuts.howToUse: Press F10 to toggle. Right-click the Polar button in the status bar to set additional tracking angles like 30° or 60°.
shortcuts.tip: Polar Tracking and Ortho Mode cannot both be active at the same time. Use Polar when drawing angled elements, and Ortho when drawing straight walls.

shortcuts.name: Object Snap Tracking Toggle
shortcuts.description: Tracks alignment paths from multiple object snap points at the same time.
shortcuts.howToUse: Press F11 to toggle. Hover over snap points to set tracking paths, then move the cursor to find intersections.
shortcuts.tip: Useful for aligning objects with each other without drawing construction lines. Works together with F3 Object Snap.

shortcuts.name: Dynamic Input Toggle
shortcuts.description: Shows dimension and angle input fields directly near the cursor while drawing.
shortcuts.howToUse: Press F12 to toggle. When ON, type a length value while drawing a line and press Tab to switch to the angle field. Press Enter to confirm.
shortcuts.tip: Dynamic Input makes it easy to draw exact lengths and angles without looking at the command line. Keep it ON for faster and more accurate drawing.

categories.description: Essential keyboard shortcuts for file management, editing, and general AutoCAD control.

shortcuts.name: New Drawing
shortcuts.description: Opens the new drawing dialog to create a new drawing file.
shortcuts.howToUse: Press Ctrl+N. Select a template from the dialog — always use acadiso.dwt for metric municipal drawings.
shortcuts.tip: Always choose acadiso.dwt when creating new drawings for municipal work in Nepal.

shortcuts.name: Open Drawing
shortcuts.description: Opens an existing drawing file from your computer.
shortcuts.howToUse: Press Ctrl+O. Browse to your project folder and select the .dwg file to open.
shortcuts.tip: You can have multiple drawings open at the same time in AutoCAD.

shortcuts.name: Save Drawing
shortcuts.description: Saves the current drawing file.
shortcuts.howToUse: Press Ctrl+S at any time to save. The first time you save a new file, it will ask you to choose a location and file name.
shortcuts.tip: Save every 10 to 15 minutes while working. This is the most important habit in AutoCAD.

shortcuts.name: Save As
shortcuts.description: Saves the current drawing with a new name or in a new location.
shortcuts.howToUse: Press Ctrl+Shift+S. Choose a new file name, location, or file format (DWG or DWT template).
shortcuts.tip: Use Save As to create a new version of a drawing, for example: FloorPlan_v1.dwg, FloorPlan_v2.dwg.

shortcuts.name: Undo
shortcuts.description: Undoes the last action. Can be pressed multiple times to undo several steps.
shortcuts.howToUse: Press Ctrl+Z once to undo the last action. Press it repeatedly to undo multiple steps going back.
shortcuts.tip: AutoCAD keeps a long undo history. You can undo many steps back. However, undo history is lost when you close the file.

shortcuts.name: Redo
shortcuts.description: Redoes an action that was previously undone.
shortcuts.howToUse: Press Ctrl+Y after using Ctrl+Z to redo what you undid.
shortcuts.tip: Redo only works immediately after an undo. Once you draw something new, the redo history is cleared.

shortcuts.name: Copy to Clipboard
shortcuts.description: Copies selected objects to the Windows clipboard for pasting into other drawings or applications.
shortcuts.howToUse: Select objects first, then press Ctrl+C. A base point will be requested to define the copy origin.
shortcuts.tip: Use Ctrl+C and Ctrl+V to copy objects between two open AutoCAD drawings.

shortcuts.name: Paste from Clipboard
shortcuts.description: Pastes objects from the Windows clipboard into the current drawing.
shortcuts.howToUse: Press Ctrl+V after copying. Click to place the pasted objects at the desired location in the drawing.
shortcuts.tip: Use Ctrl+Shift+V to paste as a block — this groups all pasted objects into one reusable block automatically.

shortcuts.name: Cut to Clipboard
shortcuts.description: Removes selected objects from the current drawing and places them on the clipboard.
shortcuts.howToUse: Select objects, then press Ctrl+X. The objects disappear from the drawing and can be pasted elsewhere.
shortcuts.tip: Cut is useful when you want to move objects from one drawing file to another.

shortcuts.name: Select All
shortcuts.description: Selects every object in the current drawing at once.
shortcuts.howToUse: Press Ctrl+A to select all visible and unlocked objects in the drawing.
shortcuts.tip: Frozen and locked layer objects are not selected by Ctrl+A. Use this to quickly move or copy the entire drawing.

shortcuts.name: Plot / Print
shortcuts.description: Opens the Plot dialog to print or export the drawing.
shortcuts.howToUse: Press Ctrl+P. Set the printer, paper size, plot scale, and plot area, then click OK to print.
shortcuts.tip: Always preview the plot before printing by clicking Preview in the Plot dialog to check the output looks correct.

shortcuts.name: Cancel / Exit Command
shortcuts.description: Cancels the current active command and returns to a neutral state.
shortcuts.howToUse: Press Escape once or twice to exit any active command. Also deselects currently selected objects.
shortcuts.tip: If AutoCAD seems stuck or unresponsive, press Escape two or three times to cancel whatever command is running.

shortcuts.name: Confirm / Repeat Last Command
shortcuts.description: Confirms the current command input. Pressing Enter on a blank command line repeats the last command used.
shortcuts.howToUse: Press Enter after typing any command or value to execute it. Press Enter when no command is active to quickly repeat the previous command.
shortcuts.tip: Pressing the Space Bar also works exactly like Enter in AutoCAD for confirming commands — many professionals prefer the spacebar because it is easier to reach.

shortcuts.name: Confirm (Same as Enter)
shortcuts.description: Works exactly like the Enter key in AutoCAD for confirming commands.
shortcuts.howToUse: Press Space Bar instead of Enter to confirm any command input. It also repeats the last command.
shortcuts.tip: Most experienced AutoCAD users use the Space Bar instead of Enter because it is faster and easier to press while drawing.

shortcuts.name: Erase Selected Objects
shortcuts.description: Deletes all currently selected objects from the drawing.
shortcuts.howToUse: Select one or more objects, then press Delete to remove them.
shortcuts.tip: This is the same as typing the ERASE command. It is faster to use the Delete key directly.

shortcuts.name: Properties Panel
shortcuts.description: Opens or closes the Properties panel showing all properties of selected objects.
shortcuts.howToUse: Select an object and press Ctrl+1. The Properties panel shows layer, color, linetype, dimensions, and all other properties of that object.
shortcuts.tip: You can change an object's layer, color, or linetype directly in the Properties panel without re-drawing it.

shortcuts.name: DesignCenter
shortcuts.description: Opens AutoCAD DesignCenter for importing blocks, layers, and styles from other drawing files.
shortcuts.howToUse: Press Ctrl+2. Navigate to another drawing file and drag its blocks, layers, or dimension styles into your current drawing.
shortcuts.tip: DesignCenter is very useful for importing standard door, window, and furniture blocks from your library into new drawings.

shortcuts.name: Sheet Set Manager
shortcuts.description: Opens the Sheet Set Manager for managing multiple drawing sheets in a project.
shortcuts.howToUse: Press Ctrl+4 to open. Used when managing a full set of municipal drawings across multiple sheets.
shortcuts.tip: Useful for large projects where you have site plan, floor plans, elevations, and sections all as separate sheets.

shortcuts.name: Command Line Toggle
shortcuts.description: Shows or hides the command line at the bottom of the screen.
shortcuts.howToUse: Press Ctrl+9 to toggle the command line on or off.
shortcuts.tip: Never hide the command line while learning AutoCAD. The command line shows you exactly what is happening and what input AutoCAD needs from you.

shortcuts.name: Clean Screen Toggle
shortcuts.description: Hides all toolbars and panels to maximize the drawing area.
shortcuts.howToUse: Press Ctrl+0 to toggle clean screen mode. Press again to restore all panels.
shortcuts.tip: Useful on small monitor screens when you need more drawing space. Press Ctrl+0 again to restore everything.

categories.description: Short command aliases for all major drawing tools. Type these in the command line and press Enter.

shortcuts.name: Line
shortcuts.description: Draws straight lines between two or more clicked points.
shortcuts.howToUse: Type L and press Enter. Click the start point, then click the end point. Continue clicking to draw connected lines. Press Escape or Enter to finish.
shortcuts.tip: Press C while in the Line command to close a polygon back to the starting point automatically.

shortcuts.name: Polyline
shortcuts.description: Draws a connected series of lines and arcs as a single object.
shortcuts.howToUse: Type PL and press Enter. Click to place points just like the Line command. All segments are one connected object.
shortcuts.tip: Use Polyline instead of Line for wall outlines and boundary lines. Because it is one object, you can offset, fillet, and edit it as a whole.

shortcuts.name: Rectangle
shortcuts.description: Draws a rectangle by specifying two opposite corner points.
shortcuts.howToUse: Type REC and press Enter. Click the first corner, then click the opposite corner. You can also type the dimensions: after the first click, type @width,height and press Enter.
shortcuts.tip: To draw an exact 3000x4500 mm room, type REC, click the first corner, then type @3000,4500 and press Enter.

shortcuts.name: Circle
shortcuts.description: Draws a circle by specifying a center point and radius.
shortcuts.howToUse: Type C and press Enter. Click the center point, then type the radius value and press Enter. Or click a second point to set the radius graphically.
shortcuts.tip: For a circular column of 300mm diameter, type C, click the center, then type 150 (radius = half of diameter) and press Enter.

shortcuts.name: Arc
shortcuts.description: Draws an arc (curved line) using three points by default.
shortcuts.howToUse: Type A and press Enter. Click the start point, click a point on the arc, then click the end point.
shortcuts.tip: Arcs are commonly used for door swing symbols in floor plans. Draw the door opening width as a line, then add the arc to show which way the door opens.

shortcuts.name: Ellipse
shortcuts.description: Draws an ellipse by defining its axis lengths.
shortcuts.howToUse: Type EL and press Enter. Click one end of the first axis, click the other end, then click or type the distance for the second axis.
shortcuts.tip: Used for round columns in plan view, decorative features, and curved architectural elements.

shortcuts.name: Spline
shortcuts.description: Draws a smooth curved line through or near a series of points.
shortcuts.howToUse: Type SPL and press Enter. Click a series of points to define the curve shape. Press Enter when done.
shortcuts.tip: Used for contour lines in site plans and irregular boundary shapes. Not commonly used in standard floor plans.

shortcuts.name: Construction Line (Xline)
shortcuts.description: Draws an infinite line extending in both directions, used as a reference or construction guide.
shortcuts.howToUse: Type XL and press Enter. Click to place the construction line. Options include Horizontal (H), Vertical (V), and Angle (A).
shortcuts.tip: Place all construction lines on a dedicated CONSTRUCTION layer and delete or freeze that layer before printing.

shortcuts.name: Ray
shortcuts.description: Draws a semi-infinite line starting at a point and extending in one direction.
shortcuts.howToUse: Type RAY and press Enter. Click the start point, then click a direction point.
shortcuts.tip: Less commonly used than Xline. Useful when you need a reference line extending in only one direction.

shortcuts.name: Polygon
shortcuts.description: Draws a regular polygon with any number of equal sides.
shortcuts.howToUse: Type POL and press Enter. Enter the number of sides, click the center point, choose Inscribed or Circumscribed, then type the radius.
shortcuts.tip: Used for hexagonal columns, octagonal features, or any regular polygon shape in architectural drawings.

shortcuts.name: Hatch
shortcuts.description: Fills a closed boundary area with a pattern to represent materials like concrete, brick, earth, or tiles.
shortcuts.howToUse: Type H and press Enter. The Hatch tab appears in the ribbon. Select a pattern (like ANSI31 for section lines), set the scale and angle, then click inside the area to fill. Press Enter to apply.
shortcuts.tip: Always set hatches on the HATCH layer. Common patterns: ANSI31 = general section, AR-CONC = concrete, AR-BRIC = brickwork, EARTH = soil/earth.

shortcuts.name: Block Definition
shortcuts.description: Creates a reusable block from selected drawing objects.
shortcuts.howToUse: Type B and press Enter. Give the block a name, click Pick Point to set the insertion base point, click Select Objects to choose what is in the block, then click OK.
shortcuts.tip: Create blocks for doors, windows, columns, north symbols, and sanitary fixtures. Blocks save time and keep drawing sizes small.

shortcuts.name: Insert Block
shortcuts.description: Inserts a previously created block or external drawing file into the current drawing.
shortcuts.howToUse: Type I and press Enter. Select the block name from the list, set the insertion point, scale, and rotation, then click OK.
shortcuts.tip: You can insert the same block many times. If you edit the original block definition, all inserted copies update automatically.

shortcuts.name: Multiline Text
shortcuts.description: Places a paragraph-style text box that can contain multiple lines and paragraphs.
shortcuts.howToUse: Type T and press Enter. Click and drag to define the text box width, then type your text. Format using the Text Editor ribbon that appears. Click outside the box to finish.
shortcuts.tip: Use Multiline Text for room names, drawing notes, specifications, and any text that needs formatting or multiple lines.

shortcuts.name: Single Line Text
shortcuts.description: Places single lines of text one at a time.
shortcuts.howToUse: Type DT and press Enter. Set the text height, rotation angle, and click a start point. Type the text and press Enter for each new line. Press Escape to exit.
shortcuts.tip: Use Single Line Text for simple labels like room numbers, level marks, and short annotations. Use Multiline Text (T) for longer notes.

shortcuts.name: Multiline Text (Alternative)
shortcuts.description: Alternative shortcut for placing Multiline Text. Same as T command.
shortcuts.howToUse: Type MT and press Enter. Functions identically to the T command.
shortcuts.tip: Both T and MT open the same Multiline Text command. Use whichever you remember more easily.

shortcuts.name: Gradient Fill
shortcuts.description: Fills a closed area with a color gradient. Used for presentation drawings.
shortcuts.howToUse: Type GR and press Enter. Select gradient type, colors, and click inside the area to fill.
shortcuts.tip: Not used in standard municipal submission drawings. More commonly used in presentation or rendered drawings.

categories.description: Shortcuts for editing and modifying existing drawing objects. These are among the most used commands in daily drafting work.

shortcuts.name: Erase
shortcuts.description: Deletes selected objects from the drawing.
shortcuts.howToUse: Type E and press Enter. Select the objects to delete and press Enter again to confirm deletion. Or select objects first, then press the Delete key.
shortcuts.tip: You can also select objects first and then press the Delete key directly — this is faster than typing E.

shortcuts.name: Move
shortcuts.description: Moves selected objects from one location to another.
shortcuts.howToUse: Type M and press Enter. Select objects and press Enter. Click the base point (where you are picking from), then click the destination point (where you want to place it).
shortcuts.tip: For precise movement, type the displacement after clicking the base point. For example, to move 500mm to the right, type @500,0 and press Enter.

shortcuts.name: Copy
shortcuts.description: Creates one or more copies of selected objects.
shortcuts.howToUse: Type CO and press Enter. Select objects and press Enter. Click the base point, then click the destination. Keep clicking to place multiple copies. Press Escape when done.
shortcuts.tip: Unlike the Move command, Copy leaves the original in place. Use it to quickly duplicate doors, windows, columns, and repeated elements.

shortcuts.name: Offset
shortcuts.description: Creates a parallel copy of a line, arc, circle, or polyline at a specified distance.
shortcuts.howToUse: Type O and press Enter. Type the offset distance and press Enter. Click the object to offset, then click on the side where you want the offset copy to appear.
shortcuts.tip: Offset is essential for creating wall thickness. Draw one wall line, then offset it by 230mm or 115mm to create the other wall face.

shortcuts.name: Mirror
shortcuts.description: Creates a mirrored copy of selected objects across a defined mirror line.
shortcuts.howToUse: Type MI and press Enter. Select objects and press Enter. Click two points to define the mirror line. Choose whether to delete the original (Yes or No).
shortcuts.tip: Use Mirror for symmetric floor plans. Draw half the floor plan, then mirror it across the centerline to complete the other half in seconds.

shortcuts.name: Array
shortcuts.description: Creates multiple copies of objects in a rectangular grid pattern or polar (circular) pattern.
shortcuts.howToUse: Type AR and press Enter. Select objects and press Enter. Choose Rectangular, Polar, or Path array type. Set the number of rows, columns, and spacing.
shortcuts.tip: Use Rectangular Array to create a column grid (evenly spaced columns in rows and columns). Use Polar Array for circular staircase arrangements.

shortcuts.name: Rotate
shortcuts.description: Rotates selected objects around a specified base point by a given angle.
shortcuts.howToUse: Type RO and press Enter. Select objects and press Enter. Click the base point (pivot point). Type the rotation angle and press Enter. Positive angles rotate counter-clockwise.
shortcuts.tip: To rotate a door from 90° to 0°, type RO, select the door, set base point at the hinge corner, type -90 and press Enter.

shortcuts.name: Scale
shortcuts.description: Enlarges or reduces selected objects proportionally.
shortcuts.howToUse: Type SC and press Enter. Select objects and press Enter. Click the base point. Type the scale factor and press Enter. 2 = double size, 0.5 = half size.
shortcuts.tip: Use the Reference option (R) when you know the current size and the desired size but not the scale factor. AutoCAD calculates the factor for you.

shortcuts.name: Stretch
shortcuts.description: Stretches or compresses part of a drawing by moving selected endpoints while keeping other endpoints fixed.
shortcuts.howToUse: Type S and press Enter. You must use a crossing window selection (right-to-left drag) to select objects. Press Enter, click the base point, then click the destination.
shortcuts.tip: Stretch is perfect for changing room dimensions. Select part of a wall crossing its endpoint, then stretch it to resize the room without redrawing.

shortcuts.name: Trim
shortcuts.description: Removes the portion of a line or object that extends past a cutting edge.
shortcuts.howToUse: Type TR and press Enter. Press Enter again to select all objects as cutting edges. Click the part of each line you want to remove.
shortcuts.tip: In newer AutoCAD versions, pressing Enter twice selects all objects as cutting edges automatically. This means you can click any line segment to trim it immediately.

shortcuts.name: Extend
shortcuts.description: Extends a line or object until it meets a boundary edge.
shortcuts.howToUse: Type EX and press Enter. Press Enter again to select all as boundary edges. Click the end of the line you want to extend toward the boundary.
shortcuts.tip: Extend and Trim work in a very similar way. In newer AutoCAD, you can switch between Trim and Extend modes by holding the Shift key while clicking.

shortcuts.name: Fillet
shortcuts.description: Rounds two lines meeting at a corner with a smooth arc of a specified radius.
shortcuts.howToUse: Type F and press Enter. Type R and press Enter to set the radius, then type the radius value. Click the first line, then click the second line to create the fillet.
shortcuts.tip: Set radius to 0 to use Fillet as a clean corner tool — it joins two non-touching lines at a sharp right-angle corner. Extremely useful for cleaning up wall junctions.

shortcuts.name: Chamfer
shortcuts.description: Creates a straight angled cut at the corner where two lines meet.
shortcuts.howToUse: Type CHA and press Enter. Set the distances (D option), then click the two lines to chamfer.
shortcuts.tip: Used for angled wall corners and architectural chamfer details. Less commonly used than Fillet in residential drawings.

shortcuts.name: Break
shortcuts.description: Breaks a line or object into two parts by removing a section of it.
shortcuts.howToUse: Type BR and press Enter. Select the object, click the first break point, then click the second break point. The section between the two points is removed.
shortcuts.tip: Use Break to create wall openings for doors and windows. Break the wall line at the opening start and end points.

shortcuts.name: Join
shortcuts.description: Joins separate but collinear or connected lines, polylines, and arcs into a single object.
shortcuts.howToUse: Type J and press Enter. Select the objects to join and press Enter. They must be touching or overlapping on the same path.
shortcuts.tip: Useful for joining wall lines that were accidentally broken or drawn as separate segments. A single joined polyline is easier to offset and edit.

shortcuts.name: Explode
shortcuts.description: Breaks a block, polyline, or grouped object into its individual component lines and arcs.
shortcuts.howToUse: Type X and press Enter. Select the block or polyline to explode and press Enter.
shortcuts.tip: Explode a door or window block only if you need to edit its internal components. After exploding, the block loses its block properties and cannot update if the original is changed.

shortcuts.name: Polyline Edit
shortcuts.description: Edits polylines — joins separate lines into a polyline, adjusts width, and more.
shortcuts.howToUse: Type PE and press Enter. Select a line or polyline. Choose options: J to Join other lines, W to set Width, F to Fit a smooth curve.
shortcuts.tip: Use PE with the Join option to convert a series of separate wall lines into a single continuous polyline. This makes offset and editing much easier.

shortcuts.name: Group
shortcuts.description: Groups multiple objects together so they can be selected and moved as one unit.
shortcuts.howToUse: Type G and press Enter. The Object Grouping dialog opens. Enter a group name, click New, select the objects, and press Enter.
shortcuts.tip: Groups are useful for grouping furniture, sanitary fixtures, or annotations that belong together but should remain individually editable.

shortcuts.name: Ungroup
shortcuts.description: Removes the grouping from grouped objects.
shortcuts.howToUse: Select the group, type UG and press Enter to ungroup.
shortcuts.tip: After ungrouping, all objects return to individual status. This cannot be undone with a simple undo in all cases.

categories.description: Shortcuts for placing all types of dimensions and annotations in drawings.

shortcuts.name: Dimension — Linear
shortcuts.description: Places a horizontal or vertical dimension line measuring a straight distance.
shortcuts.howToUse: Type DLI and press Enter. Click the first point, click the second point, then click to place the dimension line. AutoCAD auto-selects horizontal or vertical based on your cursor position.
shortcuts.tip: For municipal drawings, always use Linear Dimension for room widths, wall lengths, and overall building dimensions.

shortcuts.name: Dimension — Aligned
shortcuts.description: Places a dimension line parallel to the line being dimensioned, including diagonal dimensions.
shortcuts.howToUse: Type DAL and press Enter. Click the two points to dimension, then click to place the dimension line.
shortcuts.tip: Use Aligned Dimension for diagonal walls and angled elements where the standard horizontal/vertical linear dimension would not be correct.

shortcuts.name: Dimension — Radius
shortcuts.description: Places a radius dimension on a circle or arc.
shortcuts.howToUse: Type DRA and press Enter. Click the circle or arc, then click to position the dimension text.
shortcuts.tip: Use radius dimensions for circular columns, arcs, and curved walls.

shortcuts.name: Dimension — Diameter
shortcuts.description: Places a diameter dimension on a circle.
shortcuts.howToUse: Type DDI and press Enter. Click the circle, then click to position the dimension text.
shortcuts.tip: Use diameter dimensions for round objects like columns, manholes, and septic tanks in plan view.

shortcuts.name: Dimension — Angular
shortcuts.description: Places an angular dimension measuring the angle between two lines.
shortcuts.howToUse: Type DAN and press Enter. Click the first line, click the second line, then click to position the dimension.
shortcuts.tip: Used for angled walls, ramps, and sloped surfaces in section drawings.

shortcuts.name: Dimension — Continue
shortcuts.description: Adds a chain of continuous dimensions one after another from the last dimension endpoint.
shortcuts.howToUse: After placing a linear dimension, type DCO and press Enter. Click additional points to continue the dimension chain.
shortcuts.tip: Use Continue Dimension for chain dimensioning along walls — click each room division point to build up a complete dimension string quickly.

shortcuts.name: Dimension — Baseline
shortcuts.description: Places multiple dimensions all measured from the same baseline (origin point).
shortcuts.howToUse: After placing a linear dimension, type DBA and press Enter. Click additional points — all dimensions are measured from the same first point.
shortcuts.tip: Baseline dimensions are used in structural drawings and site plans where all measurements are referenced from a common datum point.

shortcuts.name: Leader Line
shortcuts.description: Draws a leader line with an arrowhead pointing to an object, with a text annotation attached.
shortcuts.howToUse: Type LE and press Enter. Click the arrowhead start point, click intermediate points for the leader line, then press Enter and type the annotation text.
shortcuts.tip: Use leader lines for noting materials, finishes, structural member sizes, and special conditions in section and detail drawings.

shortcuts.name: Multileader
shortcuts.description: Places a professional leader line with multiline annotation text. More powerful than the basic LE command.
shortcuts.howToUse: Type MLD and press Enter. Click the arrowhead point, then click the landing point. Type the annotation text and click outside to finish.
shortcuts.tip: Multileader is the professional standard for annotations in modern AutoCAD. It produces cleaner and more controllable leaders than the older LE command.

shortcuts.name: Tolerance
shortcuts.description: Places a geometric tolerance symbol box (used mainly in mechanical engineering drawings).
shortcuts.howToUse: Type TOL and press Enter. Fill in the tolerance values in the dialog and click OK to place.
shortcuts.tip: Not commonly used in municipal building drawings. More relevant for mechanical engineering and structural steel fabrication drawings.

shortcuts.name: Dimension Style Manager
shortcuts.description: Opens the Dimension Style Manager where you create and modify dimension styles.
shortcuts.howToUse: Type D and press Enter. Create new styles, modify existing ones, and set the current active dimension style.
shortcuts.tip: Always create a dimension style matching your print scale before starting drawing. For 1:100 scale drawings, name it DIM_100.

shortcuts.name: Edit Text
shortcuts.description: Opens a text object for editing its content.
shortcuts.howToUse: Type ED and press Enter, then click the text to edit. Or simply double-click any text object directly.
shortcuts.tip: Double-clicking directly on any text is faster than typing ED. It immediately opens the text for editing.

categories.description: Shortcuts for navigating, zooming, and controlling the drawing view.

shortcuts.name: Zoom All
shortcuts.description: Fits the entire drawing limits into the screen view.
shortcuts.howToUse: Type Z and press Enter, then type A and press Enter.
shortcuts.tip: Use Zoom All at the start of a session to see the complete working area, and after making large changes to get the full picture.

shortcuts.name: Zoom Extents
shortcuts.description: Zooms to fit all drawn objects on screen, regardless of the drawing limits.
shortcuts.howToUse: Type Z and press Enter, then type E and press Enter.
shortcuts.tip: Use Zoom Extents to find objects that have been accidentally placed far from your drawing. If your drawing looks blank, type Z, E and it will jump to where the objects actually are.

shortcuts.name: Zoom Window
shortcuts.description: Zooms into a specific area you define by drawing a rectangle.
shortcuts.howToUse: Type Z and press Enter, then type W and press Enter. Click two corners of a rectangle to define the zoom area.
shortcuts.tip: The scroll wheel on your mouse is faster for zooming in and out on specific areas.

shortcuts.name: Zoom In / Zoom Out
shortcuts.description: Zooms in or out centered on the cursor position using the mouse scroll wheel.
shortcuts.howToUse: Roll the scroll wheel forward to zoom in. Roll backward to zoom out. The zoom centers on wherever your cursor is pointing.
shortcuts.tip: This is the fastest and most natural way to zoom in AutoCAD. Use it constantly while working.

shortcuts.name: Pan
shortcuts.description: Moves the view by panning without changing the zoom level.
shortcuts.howToUse: Hold down the middle mouse button (scroll wheel) and drag the mouse to pan the view in any direction.
shortcuts.tip: This is the fastest way to pan. You never need to use the Pan command (P) when you have a scroll wheel mouse.

shortcuts.name: Pan (Command)
shortcuts.description: Activates Pan mode so you can click and drag to move the view.
shortcuts.howToUse: Type P and press Enter. Click and drag to pan. Press Escape to exit Pan mode.
shortcuts.tip: The middle mouse button drag is faster than typing P. Only use the command if your mouse has no scroll wheel.

shortcuts.name: Regen
shortcuts.description: Regenerates and redraws the entire drawing to fix display issues.
shortcuts.howToUse: Type RE and press Enter. AutoCAD redraws all objects. Circles may appear jagged — Regen fixes this.
shortcuts.tip: If circles look like polygons or lines look broken on screen, type RE to regenerate the display. This is a display issue only — the actual geometry is correct.

shortcuts.name: Hide / Show Palettes
shortcuts.description: Hides or shows all open palette panels (Properties, Layer Manager, etc.)
shortcuts.howToUse: Press Ctrl+Shift+H to toggle all open palettes on or off.
shortcuts.tip: Useful when you need to temporarily maximize the drawing area without permanently closing panels.

categories.description: Shortcuts for managing layers and object properties.

shortcuts.name: Layer Properties Manager
shortcuts.description: Opens the Layer Properties Manager panel to create, edit, and manage layers.
shortcuts.howToUse: Type LA and press Enter. Create new layers, assign colors and linetypes, and set the current layer.
shortcuts.tip: Keep the Layer Properties Manager docked as a panel on the side while drawing so you can switch layers quickly.

shortcuts.name: Properties Panel
shortcuts.description: Opens the Properties panel for the selected object, showing and allowing editing of all its properties.
shortcuts.howToUse: Select an object, then press Ctrl+1. Edit layer, color, linetype, and dimensions directly in the panel.
shortcuts.tip: If an object is on the wrong layer, select it, press Ctrl+1, and change the layer in the Properties panel. Faster than erasing and redrawing.

shortcuts.name: Match Properties
shortcuts.description: Copies the properties (layer, color, linetype, lineweight) of one object and applies them to other objects.
shortcuts.howToUse: Type MA and press Enter. Click the source object (the one whose properties you want to copy), then click all the destination objects you want to update.
shortcuts.tip: Use Match Properties to quickly fix objects on wrong layers. Click a correctly placed wall line as the source, then click incorrectly placed objects to transfer the WALL layer properties.

shortcuts.name: Lineweight Display Toggle
shortcuts.description: Turns the display of lineweights on or off in the drawing area.
shortcuts.howToUse: Type LW and press Enter. Or click the LWT button in the status bar at the bottom.
shortcuts.tip: Turn lineweight display ON to see how thick lines will print. Turn it OFF for a cleaner drawing view while working. It does not affect actual print output.

shortcuts.name: Drawing Units
shortcuts.description: Opens the Drawing Units dialog to set the measurement units and precision.
shortcuts.howToUse: Type UN and press Enter. Set Length Type to Decimal and Insertion Scale to Millimeters for all municipal work.
shortcuts.tip: Check units at the start of every new drawing session. Wrong units cause all dimensions and blocks to be incorrect.

shortcuts.name: Options
shortcuts.description: Opens the AutoCAD Options dialog for configuring program settings, display, file paths, and behavior.
shortcuts.howToUse: Type OP and press Enter. Navigate between tabs: Files, Display, Open and Save, Drafting, Selection.
shortcuts.tip: Important settings in Options: Display tab for background color and crosshair size, Drafting tab for AutoSnap and AutoTrack settings.

shortcuts.name: Purge
shortcuts.description: Removes unused layers, blocks, text styles, dimension styles, and linetypes from the drawing to reduce file size.
shortcuts.howToUse: Type PU and press Enter. The Purge dialog shows all unused items. Click Purge All to remove everything unused.
shortcuts.tip: Run Purge before saving the final version of any drawing. It significantly reduces file size and removes clutter from unused elements.

shortcuts.name: AutoCAD Audit
shortcuts.description: Checks the drawing file for errors and repairs any found.
shortcuts.howToUse: Type AUDIT and press Enter. Choose Yes to fix any errors found automatically.
shortcuts.tip: Run Audit if a drawing is behaving strangely or took a long time to open. It finds and fixes corruption in the drawing file.

categories.description: Methods for selecting objects in AutoCAD. Understanding selection is essential for efficient editing.

shortcuts.name: Window Selection
shortcuts.description: Selects only objects that are completely inside the selection rectangle. Shows as a solid blue rectangle.
shortcuts.howToUse: Click and drag from left to right to draw the selection window. Only fully enclosed objects are selected.
shortcuts.tip: Use Window Selection when you want to select only specific objects without accidentally grabbing neighboring objects.

shortcuts.name: Crossing Selection
shortcuts.description: Selects all objects that are inside or touching the selection rectangle. Shows as a dashed green rectangle.
shortcuts.howToUse: Click and drag from right to left to draw the crossing window. All objects inside and touching the boundary are selected.
shortcuts.tip: Use Crossing Selection when you want to grab everything in an area, including objects only partially inside the selection box.

shortcuts.name: Add to / Remove from Selection
shortcuts.description: Adds individual objects to an existing selection, or removes them if already selected.
shortcuts.howToUse: Hold Ctrl and click objects to add them to the current selection. Hold Shift and click to remove an object from the current selection.
shortcuts.tip: Use Ctrl+Click to build up a selection of scattered objects across the drawing without having to use a selection window.

shortcuts.name: Quick Select
shortcuts.description: Creates a selection set based on specific properties — such as all objects on a certain layer or all objects of a certain color.
shortcuts.howToUse: Type QSE and press Enter (or right-click in the drawing area and choose Quick Select). Set the property filter criteria and click OK.
shortcuts.tip: Use Quick Select to select all objects on a specific layer at once. For example, select all objects on the HATCH layer to change their scale simultaneously.

shortcuts.name: Fence Selection
shortcuts.description: Selects all objects that a drawn line (fence) crosses through.
shortcuts.howToUse: During a command that asks for object selection, type F and press Enter. Draw a line through all objects you want to select. Press Enter to confirm.
shortcuts.tip: Fence Selection is useful for selecting a row of columns, a line of dimensions, or any objects arranged in a straight path.

categories.description: Shortcuts for printing, plotting, and exporting drawings.

shortcuts.name: Plot
shortcuts.description: Opens the Plot dialog to print the current drawing to a printer or PDF file.
shortcuts.howToUse: Press Ctrl+P. Set the printer or plotter, paper size, plot area (Extents, Window, or Layout), plot scale, and plot style table. Click Preview before printing.
shortcuts.tip: Always click Preview in the Plot dialog before printing. This lets you check the scale, line weights, and sheet layout before wasting paper.

shortcuts.name: Export to PDF
shortcuts.description: Exports the drawing directly to a PDF file for sharing and municipality submission.
shortcuts.howToUse: Type EXPORTPDF and press Enter. Choose the output location and file name. Set options for sheet size and layer information.
shortcuts.tip: PDF export is the most common format for municipality submission. Export from the Layout (paper space) tab for the correctly sized sheet output.

shortcuts.name: Switch to Paper Space
shortcuts.description: Switches from Model Space to Paper Space (Layout) view.
shortcuts.howToUse: Type PS and press Enter, or click the Layout tab at the bottom of the screen.
shortcuts.tip: Use Paper Space to arrange your drawing on a standard sheet size with a title block before printing.

shortcuts.name: Switch to Model Space
shortcuts.description: Switches from Paper Space back to Model Space for drawing.
shortcuts.howToUse: Type MS and press Enter, or click the Model tab at the bottom of the screen.
shortcuts.tip: Always do your actual drawing work in Model Space. Use Paper Space only for arranging and printing layouts.

shortcuts.name: Make Viewport
shortcuts.description: Creates a viewport window in Paper Space that displays Model Space drawing content.
shortcuts.howToUse: Switch to a Layout tab, type MV and press Enter. Click and drag to draw the viewport rectangle. The model drawing appears inside it.
shortcuts.tip: Set the viewport scale precisely by selecting the viewport, then choosing the scale from the Viewport Scale dropdown in the status bar.
